user_idea: A novel CRISPR-based gene editing platform that combines base editing with epigenetic modification to simultaneously correct disease-causing mutations and regulate gene expression without creating double-strand breaks. The system uses a dual-effector approach where a cytosine base editor (CBE) is fused with a DNA methyltransferase domain (DNMT3A) to enable precise C-to-T conversions while simultaneously establishing stable epigenetic marks at target loci. This approach targets inherited metabolic disorders, specifically focusing on phenylketonuria (PKU) where both the PAH gene mutation needs correction and metabolic pathway genes require expression modulation. The platform incorporates machine learning algorithms to predict off-target effects and optimize guide RNA design for minimal collateral editing. Key innovations include: (1) a split-intein system for improved delivery efficiency in hepatocytes, (2) a reversible epigenetic switch mechanism for temporal control of gene expression, (3) integration with single-cell RNA sequencing to validate editing outcomes at cellular resolution, and (4) a biodegradable lipid nanoparticle delivery system that degrades after 72 hours to minimize immune response. The system aims to achieve >85% editing efficiency in primary human hepatocytes with <0.1% off-target activity, representing a significant advancement over current single-function base editors and traditional CRISPR-Cas9 approaches.
